name: Argentina
tagline: From the Diaguita, Guarani and Mapuche worlds of the Rio de la Plata to a Spanish viceroyalty, the independence declared at Tucuman in 1816, an age of mass immigration, the Peron era, and a hard road back to democracy after the dictatorship of 1976-1983.
summary: Argentina's history begins with the many Indigenous peoples, from the Diaguita of the northwest and the Guarani of the riverine northeast to the Mapuche and the hunter-gatherers of the Pampas and Patagonia, who inhabited the land long before Spanish ships entered the Rio de la Plata in the sixteenth century. Buenos Aires was founded in 1536, abandoned, and refounded in 1580, and for two centuries the region was a remote corner of Spanish America until the Viceroyalty of the Rio de la Plata was created in 1776. The May Revolution of 1810 set the country on the road to self-government, and on 9 July 1816 the Congress of Tucuman declared independence as the United Provinces. Decades of civil war between Buenos Aires and the provinces followed, resolved by the Constitution of 1853 and the rise of a liberal republic that drew millions of European immigrants between roughly 1880 and 1914. The twentieth century was dominated by the figure of Juan Peron and the movement he founded, and then by the military dictatorship of 1976 to 1983, whose Dirty War forcibly disappeared thousands of people and which collapsed after defeat in the 1982 Falklands/Malvinas War, opening the way to the democracy restored in 1983.

founding.label: Declaration of Independence at Tucuman
founding.detail: On 9 July 1816 the Congress meeting at San Miguel de Tucuman declared independence from Spain, proclaiming the United Provinces of South America (the United Provinces of the Rio de la Plata), the political nucleus of modern Argentina; twenty-nine delegates signed the declaration, with Francisco Narciso de Laprida presiding. The break had been prepared by the May Revolution of 25 May 1810 in Buenos Aires, which deposed the Spanish viceroy and installed the Primera Junta, the first self-governing body, after Napoleon's invasion of Spain shattered royal authority. National organisation came only decades later, when the Constitution of 1853, sanctioned at Santa Fe after the fall of Juan Manuel de Rosas, established a federal, representative and republican state and laid the legal foundation of the country.

quickFacts.label: Founding peoples
quickFacts.value: Diaguita, Guarani, Mapuche and other Indigenous nations

quickFacts.label: Buenos Aires founded
quickFacts.value: 1536 by Pedro de Mendoza; refounded 1580 by Juan de Garay

quickFacts.label: Independence
quickFacts.value: Declared 9 July 1816 at the Congress of Tucuman

quickFacts.label: Democracy restored
quickFacts.value: 10 December 1983, with the inauguration of Raul Alfonsin

eras.title: Indigenous Peoples and the Spanish Rio de la Plata
eras.standfirst: For thousands of years the land was home to many Indigenous nations, from the farming Diaguita of the northwest and the Guarani of the rivers to the Mapuche and the hunters of the Pampas and Patagonia, until Spanish expeditions reached the Rio de la Plata and founded, lost, and refounded Buenos Aires.
body: Long before any European sail appeared, the territory of present-day Argentina was inhabited by a great diversity of peoples. In the northwest, within the orbit of the Andean and later Inca worlds, the Diaguita built settled farming communities cultivating maize and other crops. In the humid northeast lived Guarani-speaking peoples, skilled farmers and rivermen, while across the vast Pampas and into Patagonia roamed semi-nomadic hunting and gathering peoples, and the Mapuche, whose influence spread eastward across the Andes, developed a resilient warrior culture that would resist outside domination for centuries.

European contact began in the early sixteenth century. The Rio de la Plata estuary was explored from 1516, and in 1536 Pedro de Mendoza founded a first settlement he named for Santa Maria del Buen Aire. Hunger and determined Indigenous resistance doomed it, and within a few years the survivors withdrew upriver to Asuncion. Spanish settlement of the region instead spread inland from Peru and Chile, founding towns such as Santiago del Estero and Cordoba in the northwest, which long remained more important than the river port.

Buenos Aires was permanently re-established only in 1580, when Juan de Garay led an expedition down from Asuncion and refounded the city. From these beginnings the Rio de la Plata became a peripheral frontier of the Spanish empire, oriented for two centuries toward the silver of Potosi and the administrative centre at Lima rather than toward the Atlantic. The Indigenous peoples who preceded the Spanish remain a deep foundation of Argentine society, surviving in its languages, place-names, and living communities.

events.title: Ancient peopling of the land
events.summary: Hunter-gatherer peoples occupy the Pampas, Patagonia and the Andean northwest over many millennia, leaving sites such as the painted hands of the Cueva de las Manos.

events.title: Diaguita and Guarani societies
events.summary: Settled Diaguita farmers flourish in the northwest within the Andean world, while Guarani-speaking peoples cultivate the riverine northeast.

events.title: Spanish reach the Rio de la Plata
events.summary: European expeditions begin to explore the great estuary of the Rio de la Plata, opening the region to Spanish ambitions.

events.title: First founding of Buenos Aires
events.summary: Pedro de Mendoza founds a first settlement on the Rio de la Plata, soon abandoned amid famine and Indigenous resistance, its survivors retreating to Asuncion.

events.title: Refounding of Buenos Aires
events.summary: Juan de Garay leads an expedition from Asuncion and permanently re-establishes Buenos Aires, fixing the future capital on the estuary.

figures.name: Pedro de Mendoza
figures.blurb: Adelantado of the Rio de la Plata who founded the first, short-lived settlement of Buenos Aires in 1536 before sickness and conflict forced its abandonment.

figures.name: Juan de Garay
figures.blurb: Leader of the expedition that refounded Buenos Aires in 1580, securing the permanent Spanish presence at the mouth of the Rio de la Plata.

pullquote.text: The land held many nations before it held a city: Diaguita, Guarani, Mapuche, and the hunters of the plains.
pullquote.attribution: On Indigenous Argentina before the conquest

eras.title: The Viceroyalty and the Colonial Era
eras.standfirst: For two centuries the Rio de la Plata was a neglected frontier of Spanish America, until the creation of the Viceroyalty of the Rio de la Plata in 1776 turned Buenos Aires into a thriving Atlantic capital whose defenders soon found they could stand without Spain.
body: Through the seventeenth and most of the eighteenth century the region remained a backwater of the Spanish empire, subordinate to the distant Viceroyalty of Peru. Trade was tightly restricted and channelled through Lima, which encouraged a flourishing contraband economy on the estuary. In the northeast, the Jesuit Order built among the Guarani a remarkable network of mission towns, the reducciones, which prospered until the Jesuits were expelled from Spanish domains in 1767, leaving the famous ruins later recognised as a World Heritage site.

The decisive change came in 1776, when King Charles III, decentralising imperial rule and seeking to defend the south against Portuguese encroachment, created the Viceroyalty of the Rio de la Plata with Buenos Aires as its capital. The new viceroyalty embraced present-day Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay and Upper Peru (Bolivia), and the silver of Potosi was now routed through Buenos Aires, transforming the port into a wealthy commercial hub with a growing, self-confident merchant and creole elite.

That new confidence was tested by the British invasions of 1806 and 1807, when British forces twice seized Buenos Aires and were twice driven out, largely by local militias organised without help from Spain. The experience proved to the people of the Rio de la Plata that they could defend and govern themselves, and it fed a rising sense of autonomy. When Napoleon's occupation of Spain in 1808 plunged the mother country into crisis, the way was open for the creoles of Buenos Aires to take power into their own hands.

events.title: Jesuit missions among the Guarani
events.summary: The Jesuit Order founds mission towns among the Guarani that flourish until the order's expulsion from Spanish lands in 1767.

events.title: Viceroyalty of the Rio de la Plata created
events.summary: Charles III establishes the new viceroyalty with Buenos Aires as capital, routing Potosi silver through the port and spurring its rise.

events.title: Free trade reforms
events.summary: Bourbon commercial reforms loosen Spain's trade monopoly, allowing Buenos Aires to trade more freely and accelerating its growth as an Atlantic port.

events.title: British invasions repelled
events.summary: British forces seize Buenos Aires twice and are twice expelled by local militias, kindling creole self-confidence and a taste for autonomy.

pullquote.text: Having beaten the British twice with their own hands, the people of Buenos Aires learned they did not need Spain.

eras.title: May Revolution and the Wars of Independence
eras.standfirst: The May Revolution of 1810 toppled the viceroy and set up the first home government; six years later the Congress of Tucuman declared independence, and the campaigns of San Martin carried the war for freedom across the Andes and into the heart of Spanish America.
body: On 25 May 1810, news that the Spanish resistance to Napoleon had collapsed reached Buenos Aires, and an open cabildo deposed the viceroy and installed the Primera Junta, the first self-governing body of the Rio de la Plata. The May Revolution did not at first proclaim independence, but it began the Argentine War of Independence and is remembered as the birth of the nation's self-government. The years that followed brought conflict between the centre at Buenos Aires and the provinces, and military campaigns to defend the revolution against royalist forces from Upper Peru.

Among the leaders of these years was Manuel Belgrano, who in 1812 raised on the banks of the Parana at Rosario the light-blue-and-white flag that became the national emblem, and who won victories at Tucuman and Salta. The decisive constitutional step came when delegates of the provinces gathered at San Miguel de Tucuman. On 9 July 1816 the Congress of Tucuman declared independence from Spain, proclaiming the United Provinces of South America; twenty-nine delegates signed, and Francisco Narciso de Laprida presided.

Independence still had to be secured on the battlefield. Jose de San Martin, the foremost soldier of the revolution, assembled the Army of the Andes at Mendoza and, in one of the great feats of the age, crossed the high cordillera in early 1817 to liberate Chile after the battles of Chacabuco and Maipu, then carried the war by sea to Peru, the citadel of Spanish power, where he was proclaimed Protector in 1821. His campaigns, alongside those of Simon Bolivar from the north, broke Spanish rule in South America. At home, however, the central authority fractured, and by 1820 the United Provinces had dissolved into a contest among rival provinces.

events.title: The May Revolution
events.summary: An open cabildo in Buenos Aires deposes the viceroy and forms the Primera Junta, the first home government, launching the war of independence.

events.title: Belgrano raises the flag
events.summary: Manuel Belgrano first flies the light-blue-and-white flag at Rosario; it is adopted by the Congress of Tucuman in 1816.

events.title: Declaration of Independence at Tucuman
events.summary: The Congress of Tucuman declares independence from Spain as the United Provinces of South America, signed by twenty-nine delegates under the presidency of Laprida.

events.title: San Martin crosses the Andes
events.summary: San Martin leads the Army of the Andes over the cordillera and defeats royalist forces at Chacabuco, opening the liberation of Chile.

events.title: Collapse of central authority
events.summary: The United Provinces fragment as central government breaks down and provincial caudillos contend for power, beginning decades of civil war.

figures.name: Jose de San Martin
figures.blurb: Argentina's foremost soldier of independence, who forged the Army of the Andes, crossed the cordillera in 1817 to free Chile, and carried the war to Peru, where he was named Protector in 1821. He is revered across the southern continent as El Libertador.

figures.name: Manuel Belgrano
figures.role: Patriot leader and creator of the flag
figures.blurb: Lawyer, economist and general who first raised the national flag at Rosario in 1812 and won the battles of Tucuman and Salta, a central figure of the independence movement.

pullquote.text: We do solemnly declare to the world that it is the unanimous and indubitable will of these provinces to break the bonds that tied them to the kings of Spain.
pullquote.attribution: From the Declaration of Independence, Tucuman, 9 July 1816

eras.title: Civil Wars, Unification and the Liberal Republic
eras.standfirst: Decades of conflict between Buenos Aires and the provinces, and between centralists and federalists, were resolved by the Constitution of 1853; in the liberal republic that followed, Argentina conquered the Pampas, exported beef and grain to the world, and drew millions of European immigrants.
body: After 1820 the United Provinces dissolved into a long struggle between Unitarians, who wanted a centralised state run from Buenos Aires, and Federalists, who defended provincial autonomy. From 1829 the Federalist caudillo Juan Manuel de Rosas dominated the country as governor of Buenos Aires, ruling with an iron hand until he was overthrown at the Battle of Caseros in 1852 by Justo Jose de Urquiza. Urquiza convened a constituent congress at Santa Fe, and in 1853 it sanctioned the National Constitution, establishing a federal, representative and republican state; after a final clash, Buenos Aires joined the union, and the country was effectively unified by 1862.

The decades that followed are remembered as the era of the liberal, oligarchic republic, often associated with the Generation of 1880. Its leaders pursued export-led growth, education, railways and European immigration. The state extended its control over the land by force: the Conquest of the Desert, the military campaign waged chiefly by Julio Argentino Roca between 1878 and 1885, brought the Pampas and northern Patagonia under national authority by killing, subduing and displacing the Mapuche and other Indigenous peoples, opening vast territories to settlement and ranching.

On those plains arose one of the world's great export economies, sending beef, wool and grain to Europe and making Argentina, by the early twentieth century, one of the richest countries on earth per head. To work and settle the land, the country threw open its doors to immigration: between roughly 1880 and 1914 some four million people, above all Italians and Spaniards but also French, Eastern Europeans and Middle Easterners, arrived and remade the nation, so that by 1914 nearly a third of the population was foreign-born and half of Buenos Aires. Pressure from this changing society led to the Saenz Pena Law of 1912, which established the secret universal male ballot and, in 1916, brought the Radical Hipolito Yrigoyen to power in the first government elected under it.

events.title: The rule of Rosas
events.summary: The Federalist caudillo Juan Manuel de Rosas dominates Argentina as governor of Buenos Aires until his fall, a period of strongman rule and conflict.

events.title: Battle of Caseros
events.summary: Justo Jose de Urquiza defeats Rosas at Caseros, opening the way to the constitutional organisation of the country.

events.title: The National Constitution
events.summary: A constituent congress at Santa Fe sanctions the Constitution of 1853, establishing a federal, representative and republican state.

events.title: The Conquest of the Desert
events.summary: Military campaigns led by Julio Argentino Roca seize the Pampas and northern Patagonia from the Mapuche and other peoples through killing, subjugation and displacement.

events.title: Mass European immigration
events.summary: Some four million immigrants, chiefly Italians and Spaniards, arrive to settle and work the land, so that by 1914 nearly a third of Argentines are foreign-born.

figures.name: Juan Manuel de Rosas
figures.blurb: Dominant figure of Argentine politics from 1829 to 1852, ruling as governor of Buenos Aires with broad powers until his defeat at Caseros and exile to England.

figures.name: Julio Argentino Roca
figures.blurb: Commander of the Conquest of the Desert against the Indigenous peoples of the south and twice president, a central figure of the Generation of 1880 and its model of export-led growth.

pullquote.text: By 1914 nearly a third of all Argentines, and half the people of Buenos Aires, had been born abroad.
pullquote.attribution: On the age of mass immigration

eras.title: The Peron Era and Political Turmoil
eras.standfirst: The twentieth century brought democratic openings, military coups, and above all the towering figure of Juan Peron, whose movement reshaped Argentina around the working class and divided the nation between Peronists and their enemies for generations.
body: The radical governments that began with Hipolito Yrigoyen in 1916 widened political participation, but the worldwide slump of 1930 triggered the first of the twentieth century's military coups and ushered in a period of fraud and instability known as the Infamous Decade. The economy turned inward, industry grew in the cities, and a new urban working class formed, providing the social base for the transformation to come.

Out of the military government of the early 1940s rose Colonel Juan Domingo Peron, who as secretary of labour built a devoted following among workers. On 17 October 1945 a great demonstration of his supporters, the descamisados, forced his release from detention, and in February 1946 he was elected president. With his charismatic wife Eva Peron, who championed the poor and won women the vote, he forged a movement, Peronism, that combined nationalism, industrialisation and sweeping social and labour reform with a tightening grip on opposition, the press and the unions. Eva's death in 1952 and economic difficulties weakened him, and in 1955 he was overthrown by the military and driven into exile.

The two decades that followed were marked by chronic instability as governments, civilian and military, tried and failed to rule with Peronism banned but its followers a majority. Amid mounting economic crisis and political violence, Peron returned in triumph in 1973 and was again elected president, but he died on 1 July 1974, leaving the presidency to his third wife, Isabel. Her government collapsed into spiralling inflation, guerrilla insurgency and right-wing terror, until the armed forces seized power on 24 March 1976.

events.title: First election under universal male suffrage
events.summary: Hipolito Yrigoyen of the Radical Civic Union wins the presidency in the first vote under the secret universal male ballot of 1912.

events.title: The first military coup
events.summary: Amid the Great Depression the army overthrows Yrigoyen, opening the fraud-ridden Infamous Decade and a century of military intervention.

events.title: Peron elected president
events.summary: Juan Peron wins the presidency with broad working-class support, launching the Peronist movement that will dominate Argentine politics.

events.title: Peron overthrown
events.summary: The Revolucion Libertadora ousts Peron, who goes into an exile of nearly two decades while Peronism is banned at home.

events.title: Peron's return and death
events.summary: Peron returns from exile and is re-elected president in 1973, but dies on 1 July 1974, leaving power to his wife Isabel amid deepening crisis.

figures.name: Juan Domingo Peron
figures.blurb: Army officer who built a mass following among workers and was elected president in 1946, 1951 and 1973. His movement, Peronism, reshaped Argentina and remains a central force in its politics half a century after his death.

figures.name: Eva Peron
figures.blurb: Known as Evita, she championed the poor and the descamisados, helped win women the vote, and became an enduring icon before her death from cancer at thirty-three.

pullquote.text: She addressed them as los descamisados, the shirtless ones, and they answered with their devotion.
pullquote.attribution: On Eva Peron and the Peronist masses

eras.title: Dictatorship, the Falklands/Malvinas, and Democratic Argentina
eras.standfirst: The military dictatorship of 1976 to 1983 waged a Dirty War that forcibly disappeared thousands of citizens; discredited by repression, economic ruin and defeat in the 1982 Falklands/Malvinas War, it gave way to a democracy that has held, despite recurring crises, ever since.
body: On 24 March 1976 the armed forces overthrew Isabel Peron and installed a junta that called itself the National Reorganization Process. Behind a promise of order it carried out a campaign of state terror, the Dirty War, against suspected leftists, activists, students, trade unionists and others. Tens of thousands were seized, held in clandestine detention centres, tortured, and murdered; many were thrown, drugged but alive, from aircraft into the sea. These victims are the desaparecidos, the disappeared. The official truth commission later documented nearly nine thousand cases, while human rights organisations estimate the dead and disappeared at around thirty thousand. In defiance of the terror, the Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo marched each week to demand the return of their children, becoming the conscience of the nation.

Weakened by economic collapse and growing protest, the junta gambled on nationalism. On 2 April 1982 it invaded the Falkland Islands, known in Argentina as the Malvinas, long claimed by the country. Britain dispatched a task force, and after ten weeks of fighting the Argentine garrison surrendered on 14 June 1982. The humiliating defeat destroyed what credibility the regime had left and forced it to call elections.

Democracy returned on 10 December 1983 with the inauguration of Raul Alfonsin. His government created the CONADEP commission, whose report Nunca Mas (Never Again) laid bare the crimes of the dictatorship, and in 1985 the Trial of the Juntas convicted its leaders, a landmark in holding a military regime to account through its own courts. The decades since have been turbulent, marked by hyperinflation in the late 1980s, the convertibility experiment of the 1990s, and the catastrophic economic and political crisis of 2001 to 2002, when the country defaulted and ran through several presidents in days. Yet through every shock, constitutional democracy has endured, and Argentina has continued the long work of remembering its disappeared and seeking justice.

events.title: Military coup and the Dirty War
events.summary: The armed forces seize power and launch the National Reorganization Process, a dictatorship whose Dirty War forcibly disappears thousands of people; from 1977 the Mothers of the Plaza de Mayo march weekly to demand their return.

events.title: The Falklands/Malvinas War
events.summary: Argentina invades the Falklands/Malvinas on 2 April; after ten weeks of war its forces surrender to Britain on 14 June, discrediting the junta.

events.title: Return to democracy
events.summary: Raul Alfonsin is inaugurated president, restoring civilian democratic rule after seven years of dictatorship.

events.title: Nunca Mas and the Trial of the Juntas
events.summary: Following the CONADEP report Nunca Mas, civilian courts try and convict the junta leaders for the crimes of the Dirty War.

events.title: Economic and political crisis
events.summary: A severe financial collapse forces a sovereign default and topples several presidents in days, the gravest crisis of the democratic era.

figures.name: Raul Alfonsin
figures.blurb: First president after the dictatorship, inaugurated in December 1983. He established the CONADEP truth commission and brought the junta leaders to trial, anchoring Argentina's return to democracy.

figures.name: Jorge Rafael Videla
figures.blurb: Army general who led the 1976 coup and headed the junta during the worst of the Dirty War. He was convicted of crimes against humanity and died in prison.

pullquote.attribution: Title of the 1984 CONADEP report on the disappeared

figures.name: Jose de San Martin
figures.blurb: Argentina's national hero, who led the Army of the Andes across the cordillera in 1817 and carried the war of independence through Chile and into Peru.

figures.name: Juan Domingo Peron
figures.blurb: The dominant political figure of twentieth-century Argentina, whose movement reshaped the country around the working class and still divides its politics.
